text: The patient is positioned in the Leo Davis position with a fifteen-degree left tilt, causing the small intestine to fall to the left side of the abdominal cavity. The assistant uses intestinal forceps to lift the terminal ileum. The surgeon separates the attachment of the terminal ileum from the posterior peritoneum, exposing the peritoneum of the ileocecal part laterally and the superior mesenteric artery trunk medially.

The surgeon enters the incision site and separates the mesocolon of the right hemicolon from the surface of the duodenum and pancreas head using both blunt and sharp dissection techniques, progressing towards the duodenal bulb.

The patient is positioned in a dorsal elevated position with a twenty-degree angle and tilted to the left by fifteen degrees to expose the gastrocolic ligament. The assistant lifts the greater curvature of the stomach, and the surgeon opens the gastrocolic ligament at the middle of the transverse colon, separating the hepatic flexure of the colon to the border of the lateral peritoneum.

The surgeon places gauze at the foot of the anterior lobe of the transverse mesocolon to mark the end of the transverse mesocolon. A horizontal incision is made at the root of the transverse mesocolon, serving as a guideline for the anatomical endpoint of the superior mesenteric vein.

The assistant elevates the mesocolon to create a forty-five degree angle with the posterior peritoneum. The surgeon incises the ileocolic mesenteric avascular zone below the ileocolic vessel and continues the incision along the superior mesenteric vein trunk to aid in locating the superior mesenteric vein.

The surgeon exposes the root of the ileocolic fascia from the right side of the superior mesenteric vein. The ileocolic vein is ligated and incised, followed by the ligation and incision of the ileocolic artery root.

The surgeon continues to dissociate the superior mesenteric vein towards the head side, noting the absence of the right colonic vein. The root of the right colic artery is ligated and incised. The patient's right colonic vein is observed to originate from the gastrocolic vein trunk.

The surgeon ligates and incises the root of the middle colic artery and the accessory middle colic vein. The middle colic vein, which flows into the superior mesenteric vein, is also ligated and incised. The colonic branch of the gastrocolic vein trunk is ligated. The dissection continues towards the right, reaching the hepatic flexure.

The surgeon dissociates the adhesion between the hepatic flexure and retroperitoneum from the surface of the genital fascia. The assistant lifts the iliopsoas muscle, allowing for sharp dissection of the lateral peritoneum around the paracolic sulcus of the ascending colon. The mesocolon of the red hemicolon is completely resected.

The patient is positioned with a dorsal elevation at a twenty-degree angle and tilted fifteen degrees to the right. The assistant lifts the gastric body as the surgeon continues to dissociate the omentum, exposing the lower edge of the pancreas. The splenic flexure of the colon is completely dissociated along the pancreatic body and tail.
The surgeon dissociates the left toes gap along the lower edge of the pancreas and proceeds to ligate and incise the inferior mesenteric vein.

The assistant lifts the mesentery near the stump of the inferior mesenteric vein, while the surgeon incises the medial boundary of the left mesocolon upwards and leftwards along the right side of the inferior mesenteric vein. The toes gap is dissociated leftwards until reaching the lateral peritoneum, and the descending colon is visibly separated from the lateral peritoneum.

The patient is positioned in the Leo Davis position with a fifteen-degree right tilt. The assistant lifts the sigmoid colon, while the surgeon dissociates the retroperitoneum from the right side of the sigmoid mesocolon. The surgeon then enters the left toes gap along the right side of the inferior mesenteric artery, exposing its root, which is subsequently ligated and incised.
The surgeon completes the dissociation of the left toes gap, ensuring careful protection of the left ureter and automatic nerve visible in the surgical field.

Gauze is placed to protect the left ureter, gonad, and iliac vessels. The assistant pushes the sigmoid colon to the right, exposing its left mesentery. The surgeon incises the left white line of Toldt and separates the sigmoid colon and descending colon from the lateral peritoneum.

The surgical team dissociates the descending colon upwards and towards the pelvic entrance downwards. They continue to dissociate the posterior rectal space downward along the toe gap, reaching the pelvic diaphragm. The anterior rectal fascia is separated from the posterior vaginal wall, maintaining the integrity of the posterior rectal mesangium. The rectum is completely dissociated by dividing the lateral rectal ligaments. The anus is resected via the perineum, and the specimen is removed completely before closing the anal area.
The surgeon sutures the pelvic peritoneum continuously using absorbable barbed wire following pneumoperitoneum reconstruction. An ileostomy is performed in the right lower quadrant.
